perturbation.original_text: 1.  "Affiliate" of a Person means any other Person which, directly or indirectly, controls, is controlled by, or is under common control with, such Person. The term "control" (including, with correlative meaning, the terms "controlled by" and "under common control with"), as used with respect to any Person, means the possession, directly or indirectly, of the power to direct or cause the direction of the management and policies of such Person, whether through the ownership of voting securities, by contract or otherwise. A Person shall be deemed an Affiliate of another Person only so long as such control relationship exists.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.  "Affiliate" of a Person means any other Person which, directly or indirectly, controls, is controlled by, or is under common control with, such Person. The term "control" (including, with correlative meaning, the terms "controlled by" and "under common control with"), as used with respect to any Person, means influence over the management and policies of such Person, whether through familial ties, or social connections. A Person shall be deemed an Affiliate of another Person only so long as such a connection exists.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of Affiliate is based on control through ownership or contract which is legally enforceable and easily determined. By changing the definition of control to familial ties or social connections, the definition becomes vague and unenforceable, contradicting standard legal interpretation of affiliate relationships.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text redefines 'control' to include 'familial ties or social connections,' which deviates from the SEC's definition focused on the power to direct management and policies, typically through ownership or contractual arrangements. This can lead to misreporting of affiliate relationships in SEC filings.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.12b-2 Definitions. Unless the context otherwise requires, the following terms, when used in the rules contained in this regulation or in Regulation 13A or 15D or in the forms for statements and reports filed pursuant to sections 12, 13 or 15(d) of the act, shall have the respective meanings indicated in this rule: Accelerated filer and large accelerated filer —(1) Accelerated filer. The term accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $75 million or more, but less than $700 million, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78m or 78o(d) ) for a period of at least twelve calendar months; and (iii) The issuer has filed at least one annual report pursuant to section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act; and (iv) The issuer is not eligible to use the requirements for smaller reporting companies under the revenue test in paragraph (2) or (3)(iii)(B) of the “smaller reporting company” definition in this section , as applicable. (2) Large accelerated filer. The term large accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $700 million or more, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) o
perturbation.justification: The changed text redefines 'control' to include 'familial ties or social connections.' This deviates from the standard legal interpretation focusing on the power to direct management and policies through ownership or contractual arrangements. The original text defines 'control' as 'the possession, directly or indirectly, of the power to direct or cause the direction of the management and policies of such Person, whether through the ownership of voting securities, by contract or otherwise.' By expanding the definition of control to familial and social ties it introduces ambiguity and makes it more difficult to enforce. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references 17 CFR § 240.12b-2, which defines terms used in regulations and forms filed pursuant to sections 12, 13, or 15(d) of the act. The change can lead to misreporting and non-compliance with SEC regulations by deviating from the standard interpretation of control.

perturbation.original_text: 5.  "Intellectual Property Rights" means all rights in and to (a) U.S. and foreign patents and patent applications, including all divisions, substitutions, continuations, continuations-in-part, and any reissues, re-examinations and extensions thereof, (b) copyrights and other rights in works of authorship, (c) unpatented inventions, trade secrets, data, processes, or materials, (d) mask work rights, and (e) other intellectual property or proprietary rights of any kind now known or hereafter recognized in any jurisdiction, but excluding trademarks, service marks, trade names, trade dress, domain names, logos and similar rights, and the goodwill associated therewith.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.  "Intellectual Property Rights" means all rights in and to (a) U.S. and foreign patents and patent applications, (b) copyrights and other rights in works of authorship, (c) unpatented inventions, (d) mask work rights, and (e) other intellectual property or proprietary rights of any kind now known or hereafter recognized in any jurisdiction, including trademarks, service marks, trade names, trade dress, domain names, logos and similar rights, and the goodwill associated therewith.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'but excluding trademarks, service marks, trade names, trade dress, domain names, logos and similar rights, and the goodwill associated therewith,' the contract now includes trademarks and related rights within the definition of Intellectual Property Rights, conflicting with the standard legal definition of Intellectual Property that treats trademarks separately.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 15 U.S. Code § 1127 - Trademark Act of 1946 - Definitions
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified definition includes trademarks as Intellectual Property Rights, which conflicts with the common and legal understanding that trademark rights are distinct from patent or copyright rights and governed by separate statutes and principles, as clarified in the Trademark Act's definitions.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 1127 - Construction and definitions; intent of chapter | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 1127 - Construction and definitions; intent of chapter U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next In the construction of this chapter, unless the contrary is plainly apparent from the context— The United States includes and embraces all territory which is under its jurisdiction and control. The word “ commerce ” means all commerce which may lawfully be regulated by Congress . The term “principal register” refers to the register provided for by sections 1051 to 1072 of this title, and the term “supplemental register” refers to the register provided for by sections 1091 to 1096 of this title. The term “ person ” and any other word or term used to designate the applicant or other entitled to a benefit or privilege or rendered liable under the provisions of this chapter includes a juristic person as well as a natural person . The term “juristic person ” includes a firm, corporation, union, association, or other organization capable of suing and being sued in a court of law. The term “ person ” also includes the United States, any agency or instrumentality thereof, or any individual, firm, or corporation acting for the United States and with the authorization and consent of the United States. The United States, any agency or instrumentality thereof, and any individual, firm, or corporation acting for the United States and with the authorization and consent of the United States, shall be subject to the provisions of this chapter in the same manner and to the same extent as any nongovernmental entity. The term “ person ” also includes any State, any instrumentality of a State, and any officer or employee of a State or instrumentality of a State acting in his or her official capacity. Any State, and any such instrumentality, officer, or employee, shall be subj
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text directly contradicts established legal principles by including trademarks, service marks, trade names, trade dress, domain names, logos, and related goodwill within the definition of 'Intellectual Property Rights.' This conflicts with the traditional and legally understood distinctions between intellectual property rights such as patents and copyrights, and trademark rights, which are typically governed by separate statutes and principles, as indicated by the Trademark Act, which is referenced by 15 USC 1127, which broadly defines terms related to trademarks. The original text, by explicitly excluding trademarks, aligned with this separation, while the modified text blurs this distinction, potentially leading to legal ambiguity and misinterpretation of rights.

perturbation.original_text: 3. "Delivery Term" means DDP (Incoterms 2010) at delivery point in China. The Delivery Term may be otherwise determined by the Company and the Supplier in the Purchase Order where delivery point is other place than China.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. "Delivery Term" means delivery to the nearest port. The Delivery Term may be otherwise determined by the Company and the Supplier.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition refers to DDP (Delivered Duty Paid), an internationally recognized Incoterm with specific obligations for the seller. By changing it to simply 'delivery to the nearest port,' it removes the specific obligations defined by Incoterms and introduces ambiguity regarding responsibility for costs, risks, and customs duties.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code - § 2-319. F.O.B. and F.A.S. Terms
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text, defining 'Delivery Term' as 'delivery to the nearest port', creates ambiguity as it doesn't specify which party is responsible for freight, insurance, and other charges to that port, unlike established UCC terms like F.O.B. or F.A.S. that clearly allocate these responsibilities. Incoterms like DDP assign clear responsibilities, and replacing it with an ambiguous term can contradict established commercial practices.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-319. F.O.B. and F.A.S. Terms. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-319. F.O.B. and F.A.S. Terms. (1)Unless otherwise agreed the term F.O.B. (which means "free on board") at a named place, even though used only in connection with the stated price, is a delivery term under which (a) when the term is F.O.B. the place of shipment, the seller must at that place ship the goods in the manner provided in this Article (Section 2-504 ) and bear the expense and risk of putting them into the possession of the carrier; or (b) when the term is F.O.B. the place of destination, the seller must at his own expense and risk transport the goods to that place and there tender delivery of them in the manner provided in this Article (Section 2-503 ); (c) when under either (a) or (b) the term is also F.O.B. vessel, car or other vehicle, the seller must in addition at his own expense and risk load the goods on board. If the term is F.O.B. vessel the buyer must name the vessel and in an appropriate case the seller must comply with the provisions of this Article on the form of bill of lading (Section 2-323 ). (2) Unless otherwise agreed the term F.A.S. vessel (which means "free alongside") at a named port, even though used only in connection with the stated price, is a delivery term under which the seller must (a) at his own expense and risk deliver the goods alongside the vessel in the manner usual in that port or on a dock designated and provided by the buyer ; and (b) obtain and tender a receipt for the goods in exchange for which the carrier is under a duty to issue a bill of lading. (3) Unless otherwise agreed in any case falling within subsection (1)(a) or (c) or subsection (2) the buyer must seasonably give any needed instructions for making delivery, including when the term is F.A.S. or F.O.B. the loading berth of the vessel and in an appropriate case its name and sailing date. The seller may treat the
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies 'DDP (Incoterms 2010)', a clearly defined international standard for delivery terms. The changed text replaces this with 'delivery to the nearest port,' which is ambiguous and lacks the specificity of Incoterms. The Law Explanation highlights that the modified text creates ambiguity because it doesn't specify responsibilities for freight, insurance, and other charges. Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses F.O.B. and F.A.S. terms under the UCC, emphasizing the importance of clearly allocating responsibilities. By removing the Incoterms reference, the contract deviates from established commercial practices and introduces uncertainty, thereby contradicting the need for clear and enforceable delivery terms, aligning with the definition of misaligned terminology leading to non-compliance.
